mappings.rationaleAI: Directly covers the organ system comprising heart, blood vessels, and blood that transports substances; addresses both invertebrate open systems and vertebrate closed systems (B.1, B.3), blood and lymph as circulating fluids (B.2), and dual pulmonary/systemic circuits.

Covers the mechanics of pulmonary ventilation as the core process of extracting oxygen and releasing CO2 (A.1), the dynamics of respiratory mechanisms (A.4), and chemoreceptor-mediated homeostatic control of breathing rate and depth (A.5).

Blood is the primary circulating fluid in vertebrates; directly covers plasma composition (B.2.b), formed elements including erythrocytes, leukocytes, and platelets (B.2.c), and lymphocytes in vertebrates (B.2.d).

Lungs are the primary vertebrate respiratory organs, covering gills-to-lungs in tetrapods (A.3.b) and the human respiratory system including alveolar gas exchange mechanics, airway structure, and dual blood supply (A.7).

The heart is the pump driving vertebrate circulatory systems; covers four-chambered vertebrate cardiac anatomy and function (B.3.b), coronary circulation (B.3.c), and biodynamics including cardiac output, heart rate, and stroke volume (B.3.e).

CO2 is the primary metabolic waste product exchanged during respiration; the article directly covers gas exchange chemistry and the challenges CO2 dissolution poses in aquatic versus terrestrial environments (A.1, A.2), as well as its role in the broader carbon cycle.

The immune system involves leukocytes and lymphocytes transported via the blood and lymph; the article covers white cell functions (B.2.c) and lymphatic system components including lymphocytes (B.2.d) in their circulatory context.

Molluscs exemplify invertebrate respiratory and circulatory diversity; the article covers gill-based respiration in aquatic molluscs (A.3.a) and open circulatory systems using haemocoel, contrasting with cephalopod closed systems (B.3.a).

Hormones are transported via the bloodstream from endocrine glands to target tissues, making them a key component of the circulating fluid medium described in B.2; the article covers plasma-borne signaling molecules and their systemic distribution.
